Both of these multiples sit well above sector medians, and what didn’t help was that big drop in 2025 revenue. So it’s a bit of a distortion, which shows how milestone-driven revenue can temporarily compress valuation multiples, and on the flip side, how their absence has now made the company appear significantly more expensive on a trailing basis.

Instead, I think it’s best to price AC Immune on its cash position and pipeline potential. They hold a market cap of about $293 million and a cash sum of CHF 91.4 million. So a substantial portion of the valuation here would be supported by its balance sheet, with the remaining enterprise value of about $183 million reflecting expectations around future clinical success. I think investors are implicitly assigning some value to upcoming Phase 2 readouts, particularly ACI-7104 in Parkinson’s and ACI-24 in Alzheimer’s. These should serve as the key inflection points moving forward. So in this context, the valuation here would suggest that the market is cautiously pricing in some probability of clinical success, but definitely not a full breakthrough. It leaves a lot of upside contingent on positive trial data.

Risks

It’s worth touching briefly on the risks here, particularly on the uncertainty surrounding ACI-7104. Even with the encouraging interim Phase 2 data and Part 1 of the VacSYn trial showing strong immunogenicity/biomarker improvements, we have to remember that these findings are based on a relatively small dataset. It was also done over a short observation period. So my concern is that in neurodegenerative diseases, early biomarker or clinical signals sometimes fail to translate into durable disease-modifying effects in larger/longer trials. So that leaves a meaningful-sized risk that the final mid-2026 readout may not again confirm the slowing of disease progression. And even if results remain positive, they may not actually be robust enough to shift the standard of care or justify premium pricing.

A second risk I see here would be in the regulatory and development pathway. What we know is that the company intends to use the interim data to engage with regulators and potentially advance ACI-7104 into a pivotal Phase 3 trial. But there is no guarantee that regulatory agencies will agree that the current evidence is sufficient to support such progression. The risk here is that regulators may require additional Phase 2 data, and a much longer follow-up, or alternative endpoints. These, while not fatal to the candidate's trajectory, would definitely introduce a delay to timelines and increase costs.

Looking ahead

As for what’s next, the key focus for investors here should be clinical catalysts, but also their cash management. The most important near-term event is the final Phase 2 Part 1 readout for ACI-7104, expected in mid-2026. This should be critical in validating whether the interim signals can actually translate into meaningful disease modification. Now, other than ACI-7104, we also have the interim data from the ACI-24 ABATE trial in Alzheimer’s sometime in 1H26, as well as early readouts from the ACI-19764 Phase 1 study sometime in the latter half of this year.

So there are a number of upcoming data readouts this year that could all impact the stock. Ultimately, AC Immune’s trajectory over the next 12 to 18 months is going to be driven by whether it can convert the promising early data into late-stage validation. With a key challenge being maintaining sufficient capital to reach those inflection points.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - March 2026
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. The regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. The emphasis here is on high growth rather than high yield. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than in their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook

Since Nov. 2025, the market has been mostly trading in a tight range between S&P 500 (SPX) levels 6600 and 7000. There have been so many developments in the last month or so. The issue that is causing the most concern to the market is the ongoing Iran war and the resulting surge in oil prices. This has moved the market closer to SPX6600 rather than SPX7000. There is a lot of uncertainty in the market, and it is difficult to predict how long the Iran conflict is going to last. The longer it drags, the more pain is expected for the energy prices, inflation, and the world economy. This will also likely tie the hands of the Fed, and any further interest-rate cuts may be off the table for now.

On top of that, the other worries, such as AI overvaluations and overborrowing, the fear of the AI impacts on software stocks, and the broader overvaluation of U.S. stocks in comparison with the rest of the world, are still there but have been pushed to the background.

You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

All that being said, we can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. We intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

Selection Criteria for High-Growth Dividend Stocks:

Note: For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article). This section is common among articles under this series. The regular readers could skip this section.

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth)
  • Payout ratio (dividend per share divided by EPS)
  • Past three years and five-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-year dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/capital and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth during the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95% but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 3-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score".

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks:

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 3-year dividend growth. This is to account for more recent growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years)

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (3 years)

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with the Highest EPS and RS Rating

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: AU, CCJ, COKE, FIX, LRCX, TRGP, TSM, and WWD (8 duplicates).

Appeared three times: HWM (2 duplicates)

Appeared four times: VRT (3 duplicates).

We are now left with 37 (50 - 13) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is one on the list. We remove GE. There are also two others that don't have the sales growth figures readily available, but we will give them the benefit of the doubt and keep them for now. Now, we have 36 names left.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes WIT, AEG, PGR, BSY, and MSFT. We now have 31 (36-5) names.

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. We have no such case. We still have 31 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 60. There are none in our list. However, we remove STRL as it pays no dividends. We are now left with 30 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major:

Finance-Bank-Foreign: We keep CIB but remove BBVA and NWG.

Finance-Regional Banks: We remove BK.

Financial Services and Misc: We keep IBKR but remove CBOE.

Financial- Insurance: We keep CB.

Computer Software:

Computer Electronics: We will keep APH and VRT.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep LRCX but remove TSM.

Consumer-Desc.: We keep TPR, but remove RL and TJX.

Industrial- Aerospace & Defense: We keep HWM and WWD

Industrial- Const. & Engineering: We keep TS.

Industrial- Transportation:

Materials-Gold: We keep AU, but remove KGC and B (Barrick Mining).

Energy-Oil & Gas: We keep CVE and TRGP, but remove PR.

Utility: We keep NFG.

In all, we removed seven names at this stage, so we are finally left with 20 (30-10) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector/segment):

(HWM), (WWD), (FIX), (CIB), (CCJ), (TPR), (VIV), (AXIA), (VRT), (APH), (IBKR), (AU), (CB), (LLY), (CVE), (TRGP), (LRCX), (COKE), (TS), and (NFG).

Table 2: Top 20 High-Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at our wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is 'not' a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be on the lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (February 2026):

(WWD), (FIX), (CCJ), (TPR), (VRT), (AU), (CVE), (LRCX), (COKE), and (TS).

Previous Month Selections:

(WWD), (FIX), (CIB), (CCJ), (TPR), (STRL), (VRT), (AU), (CVE), and (LRCX).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. This month, the turnover is quite low as only two (out of ten) stocks are being replaced.

If a stock from the previous month has been dropped or replaced, it does not mean that the stock is no longer attractive. It simply means that our filtering process has found something more appealing in terms of momentum if two stocks are at par qualitatively.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Performance

After several months of very strong successive gains, the portfolio growth came out negative this past month. This has been mainly due to the overall market feeling some pain due to the Iran war situation and the resulting surge in oil prices.

Backtesting, in this case, may not be highly useful, but it does give some rough idea of how an HG portfolio (as described in this article) should perform in comparison to our benchmarks, the S&P500 and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund (VIG). We are presenting the backtesting from January 2020 to February 2026. In fact, the period is rather short for any meaningful backtesting, but at least two of the stocks (in our selection) do not have a long history. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year.

It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 10 years or even 5 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise and should be viewed with a grain of salt. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023

This series was started in January 2023. Since then, we have provided the progress and performance of the series as it progressed. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), and also with the S&P 500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of January 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks:

This past month, the performance of our HG-portfolio was negative by -2.02% in line with the overall market. Also, the portfolio has completed 38 months (or 3 years plus), and the portfolio has returned close to 27.6% on an annualized basis, while the S&P500 returned 18% annualized in the same period. On a cumulative basis, the performance was 117% versus 70%, respectively.

This past month's performance was pulled down mainly by AngloGold Ashanti (AU) and Grupo Cibest S.A (CIB), each down by 10% plus. On the positive side, there was only one stock, Vertiv (VRT), that returned a gain in excess of 10%. Also, we had good performance from Comfort Systems (FIX) and Cenovus Energy (CVE).

For the next month, we are retaining eight stocks from the previous month and replacing only two stocks, which is unusually high. Some of the choices were difficult to make as they were at par, for example, AngloGold versus Kinross Gold (KGC); however, we kept the existing position in AU. Similarly, electronic components company, Vertiv, and Amphenol (APH) are equally appealing, but we kept VRT from the last month's position. We eliminated Sterling Infrastructure (STRL) and Grupo Cibest S.A. from last month and replaced them with Coca-Cola Consolidated (COKE) and Tenaris (TS). The rest of the positions from last month will continue this month.

As can be seen in Table-3, the final portfolio of 10 stocks is highly diversified into many different sectors and industry segments.

Please note that our list changes from month to month. These are not "buy" recommendations as such, but a short list of outstanding dividend-growth stocks to research further.

The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we should expect them to have high volatility as well, especially when the market appears to be overvalued. We recommend due diligence.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
